editorial: With only 9% cloud cover and a last quarter moon at 34% illumination, tonight offers exceptional astrophotography conditions with minimal light pollution interference. The recent M1.5 solar flare won't produce aurora where you are, but it underscores an active sun worth monitoring as geomagnetic activity remains unsettled. Your real opportunity is wide-open skies and a moon dark enough to let deep-sky targets shine, making this an ideal night to image nebulae, clusters, or galaxies if you have equipment ready. Rain is shutting down observing tonight across most regions, so put the telescope away. A C2.7 solar flare fired off recently and Falcon 9 just launched Starlink Group 10-42, but neither will be visible through cloud cover. The Perseids arrive in 33 days, so tonight is a wash but the real show is still weeks out. Check your Shoot Score and Your Sky Tonight for how conditions actually break at your location.

With just 12% cloud cover and an astrophotography score of 7.3/10, tonight ranks as genuinely excellent for imaging, and the waning crescent moon at 16% illumination keeps the sky dark without washing out faint objects. The recent C6.0 solar flare signals activity on the sun, though aurora odds remain zero for most observers tonight, so focus instead on deep-sky work, planets, and that razor-thin lunar crescent if it clears your horizon. The Perseids arrive in 32 days, but tonight's pristine conditions are rare enough to warrant setting up if you can; clear skies like this often don't repeat for weeks. With 3% cloud cover and an astrophotography score of 7.8/10, tonight ranks among the best clear-sky opportunities this month for imaging deep sky objects and the waning crescent moon. The recently-launched Falcon 9 Block 5 carrying Starlink Group 17-48 will pass overhead within hours of sunset depending on your latitude, offering a bright, trackable pass for anyone with binoculars or a camera. A C6.0 solar flare earlier today signals continued activity on the sun, so monitor NOAA's space weather alerts for any shifts in aurora probability over the next 48 hours as solar wind arrives. The Perseids are 31 days out, so tonight is a dress rehearsal for the gear and locations you'll use when that shower peaks in August.
